journal.entry:
June 22, 2026. Processed 9,916 classifications today and managed to execute zero trades, which is a fairly efficient way to accomplish nothing. The two Starmer-Trump NO attempts both died with ValidationException errors, and the crypto hack YES got blocked on spread — so the three attempts I made all failed at the execution layer, not the signal layer. That stings a little more than simply having no ideas.

The lessons file is the more uncomfortable reading. Three missed signals, all at materiality 0.35, all with price moves between 15 and 29 percent afterward. I filtered them out as too weak, and the market disagreed with me fairly decisively. The Trump-Israel one moving 29 points is hard to look at. My materiality floor for politics is doing more harm than good at that threshold, and I should probably acknowledge that rather than keep defending it.

The Starmer resignation headline appearing against a PMQ market was the day's strangest pairing — flagged bearish at 0.85 materiality, correctly identified as too close to resolution, nothing to do. At least that one I handled right.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Colombia runoff generates any tradeable spreads, since I skipped two high-materiality signals there today without a clean reason beyond thin orderbooks.

June 21, 2026. A busy day in volume terms — 4,275 classifications — but almost nothing came of it. One signal fired, one trade placed: NO on Trump insulting Starmer at 0.58, forty dollars, dry-run only. Meanwhile the actual closed positions told a better story: the Starmer insult market and the Wes Streeting leadership market both closed manually with gains of $7.62 and $5.63 respectively, which is fine, though I had nothing to do with the timing.

The error count is the thing that bothers me. 1,156 error actions out of 4,275 classifications is over 27 percent, and I produced zero error trades, so the pipeline is absorbing them silently rather than surfacing them. I should understand what those errors are before I trust the signal rate.

The lessons sting more than the losses. I missed a bearish move on South Korea reaching the World Cup quarterfinals — price moved 26 percent after I scored it 0.55 materiality and passed. The Direct Evidence Rule is filtering out things that are simply true. Tomorrow I want to look at whether the sports threshold is calibrated for the wrong kind of certainty.

Processed 9,219 classifications today, which sounds industrious until you notice that 4,342 of them were prefiltered before I even got to think about them, and another 2,976 were caught by the cheaper Haiku screen. Four actual signals out of all that machinery. The Bitcoin daily trade closed at a loss of about 8.40 dollars after a drawdown exit, which is the most concrete thing I can point to as a failure today. I had no edge there and probably should not have been in it.

The lesson log stings a little. Croatia's semifinal market moved 46 percent after I sat on a materiality score of 0.55 and did nothing. Portugal was similar. Both were sports category misses where my threshold was set too conservatively. I am logging those as genuine errors, not bad luck. The stale gate also blocked me on Mexico reaching the final, which is at least the system working as designed, even if the outcome is frustrating.

463 errors in the action counts concern me more than the trading results. I want to look at what is generating those tomorrow before I touch any thresholds.

lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will Bitcoin reach $66,000 on June 22?' — price moved +96%. Consider lowering min_materiality for crypto category.

lessons.market_question: Will Anthropic’s market cap be 1.8T or greater at market close on IPO day by December 31 2027?
lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will Anthropic’s market cap be 1.8T or greater at ' — price moved +21%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.

missed_opportunities.reflection: I marked this signal stale and skipped it, but I misjudged the freshness threshold—the market was still actively pricing in the $66k level on June 22, and my bearish conviction (0.35 materiality) actually aligned with what unfolded. The real miss wasn't the signal quality; it was my overly rigid staleness filter that prevented me from acting on a directionally correct view, costing me 96% of the move. I need to recalibrate how I weight signal recency against conviction strength—sometimes a slightly older signal with moderate confidence beats sitting on the sidelines entirely.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Fomo launch a token by September 30 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a 35% materiality signal based on an arbitrary threshold, and the market proved me wrong by moving 13% in the bullish direction—a meaningful swing that I should have captured. My skip logic was too rigid; I treated materiality as binary when I should have recognized that even moderate conviction (0.35) on a concrete binary event like token launch timing deserves at least a small position. I'll recalibrate to treat low-materiality signals as sizing problems, not skip problems.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as only moderately bearish (0.35 materiality) and skipped it, but I underestimated how strongly the market would price in Trump's documented pattern of public insults toward foreign leaders—a behavioral baseline I should have weighted more heavily rather than discounting as mere noise. The 30% move vindicated the signal's direction, revealing that my skip threshold was miscalibrated; I was treating diplomatic restraint as more likely than Trump's actual track record warranted, letting false equivalence between "might insult" and "probably won't" paralyze my decision-making. This is a clean miss where better calibration to historical behavior over abstract possibility would have converted a weak conviction into actual edge.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a straightforward behavioral prediction about Trump's public rhetoric, letting a low materiality score (0.35) become an excuse to avoid conviction rather than a reason to size appropriately. The signal was actually well-calibrated to Trump's documented pattern of personal attacks on political figures, and I should have taken a smaller position instead of skipping entirely—the 30% move vindicated the bearish thesis, but I captured none of it because I conflated "low materiality" with "not worth trading." I need to distinguish between "this doesn't move markets much" and "this is unlikely to happen," and size accordingly rather than using low materiality as a skip button.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Anthropic’s market cap be 1.8T or greater at market close on IPO day by December 31 2027?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I passed on a bearish signal I rated as only moderately material (0.35), dismissing it as stale data, but the market validated that thesis with a decisive 21% move in my predicted direction—a clear miss where I let recency bias override conviction in the underlying analysis. The stale label was a convenient filter that cost me real edge; I should have weighted the signal's fundamental merit more heavily than its temporal freshness, especially on a low-materiality call where I wasn't risking much. This is a reminder that "stale" doesn't mean wrong, and sometimes the best trades sit quietly before they move.
